root_text.sn47.6:1.1: “Bhūtapubbaṁ, bhikkhave, sakuṇagghi lāpaṁ sakuṇaṁ sahasā ajjhappattā aggahesi.
root_text.sn47.6:1.2: Atha kho, bhikkhave, lāpo sakuṇo sakuṇagghiyā hariyamāno evaṁ paridevasi:
root_text.sn47.6:1.3: ‘mayamevamha alakkhikā, mayaṁ appapuññā, ye mayaṁ agocare carimha paravisaye.
root_text.sn47.6:1.4: Sacejja mayaṁ gocare careyyāma sake pettike visaye, na myāyaṁ, sakuṇagghi, alaṁ abhavissa, yadidaṁ—yuddhāyā’ti.
root_text.sn47.6:1.5: ‘Ko pana te, lāpa, gocaro sako pettiko visayo’ti?
root_text.sn47.6:1.7: Atha kho, bhikkhave, sakuṇagghi sake bale apatthaddhā sake bale asaṁvadamānā lāpaṁ sakuṇaṁ pamuñci:
root_text.sn47.6:1.8: ‘gaccha kho tvaṁ, lāpa, tatrapi me gantvā na mokkhasī’ti.
root_text.sn47.6:2.1: Atha kho, bhikkhave, lāpo sakuṇo naṅgalakaṭṭhakaraṇaṁ leḍḍuṭṭhānaṁ gantvā mahantaṁ leḍḍuṁ abhiruhitvā sakuṇagghiṁ vadamāno aṭṭhāsi:
root_text.sn47.6:2.2: ‘ehi kho dāni me, sakuṇagghi, ehi kho dāni me, sakuṇagghī’ti.
root_text.sn47.6:2.3: Atha kho sā, bhikkhave, sakuṇagghi sake bale apatthaddhā sake bale asaṁvadamānā ubho pakkhe sannayha lāpaṁ sakuṇaṁ sahasā ajjhappattā.
root_text.sn47.6:2.4: Yadā kho, bhikkhave, aññāsi lāpo sakuṇo ‘bahuāgato kho myāyaṁ sakuṇagghī’ti, atha tasseva leḍḍussa antaraṁ paccupādi.
root_text.sn47.6:2.5: Atha kho, bhikkhave, sakuṇagghi tattheva uraṁ paccatāḷesi.
root_text.sn47.6:2.6: Evañhi taṁ, bhikkhave, hoti yo agocare carati paravisaye.
root_text.sn47.6:3.1: Tasmātiha, bhikkhave, mā agocare carittha paravisaye.
root_text.sn47.6:3.2: Agocare, bhikkhave, carataṁ paravisaye lacchati māro otāraṁ, lacchati māro ārammaṇaṁ.
root_text.sn47.6:3.3: Ko ca, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno agocaro paravisayo?
root_text.sn47.6:3.6: Cakkhuviññeyyā rūpā iṭṭhā kantā manāpā piyarūpā kāmūpasaṁhitā rajanīyā,
root_text.sn47.6:3.10: kāyaviññeyyā phoṭṭhabbā iṭṭhā kantā manāpā piyarūpā kāmūpasaṁhitā rajanīyā—
root_text.sn47.6:4.1: Gocare, bhikkhave, caratha sake pettike visaye.
root_text.sn47.6:4.2: Gocare, bhikkhave, carataṁ sake pettike visaye na lacchati māro otāraṁ, na lacchati māro ārammaṇaṁ.
root_text.sn47.6:4.3: Ko ca, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno gocaro sako pettiko visayo?
root_text.sn47.6:4.7: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu kāye kāyānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ;
root_text.sn47.6:4.10: dhammesu dhammānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ—
root_text.sn47.6:4.11: ayaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno gocaro sako pettiko visayo”ti.

translation_text.sn47.6:1.1: “Once upon a time, mendicants, a hawk suddenly swooped down and grabbed a quail.
translation_text.sn47.6:1.2: And as the quail was being carried off he wailed,
translation_text.sn47.6:1.3: ‘I’m so unlucky, so unfortunate, to have roamed out of my territory into the domain of others.
translation_text.sn47.6:1.4: If today I’d roamed within my own territory, the domain of my fathers, this hawk wouldn’t have been able to beat me by fighting.’
translation_text.sn47.6:1.5: ‘So, quail, what is your own territory, the domain of your fathers?’
translation_text.sn47.6:1.6: ‘It’s a ploughed field covered with clods of earth.’
translation_text.sn47.6:1.7: Confident in her own strength, the hawk was not daunted or intimidated. She released the quail, saying,
translation_text.sn47.6:1.8: ‘Go now, quail. But even there you won’t escape me!’
translation_text.sn47.6:2.1: Then the quail went to a ploughed field covered with clods of earth. He climbed up a big clod, and standing there, he said to the hawk:
translation_text.sn47.6:2.2: ‘Come get me, hawk! Come get me, hawk!’
translation_text.sn47.6:2.3: Confident in her own strength, the hawk was not daunted or intimidated. She folded her wings and suddenly swooped down on the quail.
translation_text.sn47.6:2.4: When the quail knew that the hawk was nearly there, he slipped under that clod.
translation_text.sn47.6:2.5: But the hawk crashed chest-first right there.
translation_text.sn47.6:2.6: That’s what happens when you roam out of your territory into the domain of others.
translation_text.sn47.6:3.1: So, mendicants, don’t roam out of your own territory into the domain of others.
translation_text.sn47.6:3.2: If you roam out of your own territory into the domain of others, Māra will find a vulnerability and get an opportunity.
translation_text.sn47.6:3.3: And what is not a mendicant’s own territory but the domain of others?
translation_text.sn47.6:3.4: It’s the five kinds of sensual stimulation.
translation_text.sn47.6:3.6: Sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing.
translation_text.sn47.6:3.10: Touches known by the body, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing.
translation_text.sn47.6:3.11: This is not a mendicant’s own territory but the domain of others.
translation_text.sn47.6:4.1: You should roam inside your own territory, the domain of your fathers.
translation_text.sn47.6:4.2: If you roam inside your own territory, the domain of your fathers, Māra won’t find a vulnerability or get an opportunity.
translation_text.sn47.6:4.3: And what is a mendicant’s own territory, the domain of the fathers?
translation_text.sn47.6:4.5: It’s the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.
translation_text.sn47.6:4.7: It’s when a mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.sn47.6:4.8: They meditate observing an aspect of feelings …
translation_text.sn47.6:4.10: principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.sn47.6:4.11: This is a mendicant’s own territory, the domain of the fathers.”

variant_text.sn47.6:1.7: asaṁvadamānā lāpaṁ sakuṇaṁ pamuñci → avacamānā amuñci (bj), avādamānā lāpaṁ sakuṇaṁ pamuñci (sya-all)
